--- SOURCE 1: New Technology Enables Better Human-Robot Collaboration ---
URL: https://www.assemblymag.com/articles/99374-new-technology-enables-better-human-robot-collaboration

SUMMARY:
The illuminated blue safety lines dynamically adjust to the machine's movements, enabling safe interaction between humans and robots.

FULL CONTENT:
### Assembly Lines

# New Technology Enables Better Human-Robot Collaboration

![blue safety lines](//www.assemblymag.com/ext/resources/issues2/2025/07-jul/asb0725lines1-forweb.webp?1752545309)

New safety technology generates visible light curtains around the area where a robot is working. The illuminated blue safety lines dynamically adjust to the machine’s movements, enabling safe interaction between humans and robots.

*Photo courtesy Fraunhofer IFF*

MAGDEBURG, Germany—Engineers at the Fraunhofer Institute for Factory Operation and Automation (IFF) here have developed new technology that ensures safety in AI-generated robot movements and enables better [human-machine collaboration](/keywords/2823-human-robot-collaboration).

The technology, called PARU, uses advanced cameras and projectors to display visible warning and protective fields directly around a robot and recognize when people enter the safety zones. After the projector and the two cameras are calibrated, virtual expectation images are generated as the first step.

Then, the system projects a visible light curtain around the robot and the component that is to be picked up in accordance with the distance formula set out in the ISO/TS 15066 standard.

“This light curtain acts as a safety line, visualizing for employees the protective space that humans must keep clear,” says Norbert Elkmann, Ph.D., head of the robotic systems department at Fraunhofer IFF. “If any part of a body comes into contact with the line, [work] is interrupted. The cameras recognize that there is a discrepancy between what they expect to see and the real-world image.

“Depending on the situation, the robot halts its movement right away or slows its speed,” explains Elkmann. “The safety areas are adjusted dynamically to the machine’s movements, as PARU always considers the robot’s current status, making it ideal for use in cognitive robotics.

“Our technology is unique,” claims Elkmann. “No other system allows for a smaller distance between humans and robots, while observing the specifications set by the applicable standards and also needing so little space. This is possible because the cameras and sensors recognize not only torsos, arms and heads, but even fingers.”

Another advantage is that the projection can also show people where the robot will be moving as its next step, further enhancing trust in working with machines. The additionally coded visible safety lines work independently of ambient lighting angles and conditions. If the cameras or projectors stop working, the entire system is automatically switched off.

Elkmann and his colleagues also recently developed a computer-aided system that makes human-robot collaboration applications efficient, cost-effective and safe.

“Digital assistants support the risk assessment and safety approval processes, and make it easier for new entrants in particular to comply accurately and efficiently with the full range of obligations under the EU’s Machinery Directive,” says Elkmann. “Unlike the collision measurement feature, the safety approval tool works entirely digitally. It takes parameters such as collision force and pain threshold into account to determine the robot’s maximum permitted speed.

“The modules can optionally be incorporated into any kind of robot controls or existing simulation environments for planning purposes to precisely align economic specifications with applicable safety requirements,” explains Elkmann. “This prevents planning errors and saves on engineering costs.”

Review article

Advancements in AI-enhanced collaborative robotics: towards safer, smarter, and human-centric industrial automation
===================================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Ronit Shah a, Arockia Selvakumar Arockia Doss a, Natrayan Lakshmaiya b

Show more

Outline

Add to Mendeley

Share

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rineng.2025.105704](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.rineng.2025.105704 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S259012302501775X&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •AI integration enhances cobots' adaptability, safety, and human collaboration. 
*   •Collaborative robots improve efficiency across manufacturing, healthcare, and logistics. 
*   •Emerging technologies like digital twins and IoRT expand cobots' industrial applications. 
*   •Safety innovations enable seamless human-cobot interaction without physical barriers. 
*   •Future trends emphasize cognitive adaptability and human-centric automation (Industry 5.0). 

Abstract
--------

Traditional industrial robots have long been central to manufacturing automation; however, limitations such as inflexible programming, safety concerns in human-robot interaction, and poor adaptability to dynamic production needs have restricted their widespread application. Collaborative robots (cobots) are reshaping this paradigm by enabling safe, efficient, and intelligent human-robot collaboration. This review examines the evolution and integration of collaborative robots (cobots) with Artificial Intelligence (AI), machine learning, and smart sensing technologies to enhance safety, adaptability, and human-centric automation. The paper explores how AI-enhanced cobots optimize production efficiency by reducing cycle times, improving product quality, and facilitating adaptive manufacturing in the automotive, healthcare, and logistics sectors. It highlights critical safety innovations like force-limiting systems, speed and separation monitoring, and ergonomic human-robot interaction. Furthermore, emerging concepts such as digital twins, blockchain for cybersecurity, and Internet of Robotic Things (IoRT) are examined for their potential to advance cobot functionality. Challenges including high initial costs, technical complexity, and cybersecurity threats are critically analyzed. Future trends, including cognitive adaptability, personalized interaction, and the shift toward Industry 5.0′s human-centered approach, are also explored. This study identifies key opportunities and challenges in collaborative robotics and proposes strategic pathways for fostering safer, smarter, and more flexible industrial environments.

*   [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123025018456)
*   [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123025018018)

Keywords
--------

Collaborative Robots (Cobots)

Human-Robot Collaboration (HRC)

Artificial Intelligence in Robotics

Industry 5.0

Human-Centric Automation

1. Introduction
---------------

Automation is transforming industries, and collaborative robots are at the forefront, enabling safe and efficient human-machine collaboration. In contrast to traditional robots, which are restricted to isolated tasks, cobots work alongside humans, utilizing advanced sensors, force limiters, and user-friendly interfaces. They address issues of labor shortages, customization requirements, and safety concerns. Easy to implement and lightweight, cobots are ideal for tasks that require a combination of human intuition and robot accuracy, such as assembly and inspection. Apart from production, cobots are also being implemented in healthcare, agriculture, and logistics sectors to address some of the operations that pose difficulties. Cobot usage ventures into Industry 5.0, the era of human-centric automation. Industry 4.0 emphasizes connectivity and decision-making informed by data-driven databases. Industry 5.0 integrates human well-being with smart systems, placing cobots at the core of human-centric workplaces. This review focuses on the development of collaborative robots, also known as cobots, emphasizing their integration with artificial intelligence (AI) to enhance safety, efficiency, and human-centricity. The review examines design, sensor, and machine learning-based safety system developments, with a focus on their application in minimizing risk and enhancing usability in collaborative environments. The efficiency contribution of cobots is also exemplified through the minimization of cycle times, enhancement of product quality, and optimization of processes in the automobile manufacturing and logistics sectors. It also highlights their human-friendly design, which helps workers with diligence, reduces fatigue and fosters collaboration. Finally, the review also highlights pitfalls, such as exorbitantly high start-up costs, compatibility concerns, and cybersecurity risks, while examining emerging trends, including AI-facilitated adaptive learning, digital twins, and blockchain. These technologies can further fill gaps and enhance the functionality of cobots, enabling their widespread use across various industries. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#fig0001) illustrates a simulation model of an innovative manufacturing environment where cobots seamlessly interact with automated systems to optimize production processes.

![Image 11: Fig 1:](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S259012302501775X-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (1MB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S259012302501775X-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (1MB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S259012302501775X-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Simulation of a Smart Manufacturing Plant Integrating Cobots and Automated Systems.

2. Methodology
--------------

A systematic literature review was conducted to examine the multidisciplinary landscape of collaborative robots. A review of publications from 1998 to 2025 examined technological advances, safety implications, applications of collaborative robotics, and emerging trends in the field of collaborative robotics.

### 2.1. Databases and sources

The following academic databases and digital libraries were searched: IEEE Xplore, ScienceDirect, SpringerLink, ACM Digital Library, MDPI, Taylor & Francis, Emerald Insight, and Google Scholar.

### 2.2. Search terms and strategy

A combination of keywords and Boolean operators was used to ensure comprehensive coverage. Core terms included:
*   •“collaborative robot” OR “cobot” 
*   •“human-robot collaboration” OR “HRC” 
*   •“cobot safety” OR “power and force limiting” OR “speed and separation monitoring” 
*   •“AI in collaborative robotics” OR “AI-powered cobots” 
*   •“cobot applications” OR “medical cobots” OR “cobot in healthcare” OR “cobot in manufacturing” 
*   •“hand guiding cobots” OR “AI-guided hand programming” 
*   •“Universal Robots UR5” OR “UR5e” OR “DLR-KUKA” 
*   •“smart factories” OR “Industry 4.0″ OR “Industry 5.0″ OR “IoRT” 
*   •“robot operating system” OR “ROS” OR “ROS 2″ OR “Simulink-based cobot programming” 
*   •“human-centric automation” OR “cognitive cobots” OR “adaptive collaboration” 

These terms were tailored per database and used in various combinations to target studies relevant to both academic and industrial contexts.

### 2.3. Screening and selection process

A two-stage screening process was employed:
*   1.**Title and Abstract Screening** Initially, all retrieved records underwent a preliminary screening based on titles and abstracts. This stage aimed to exclude studies that were irrelevant to the scope of physical human-robot interaction (pHRI), collaborative robotics, or industrial AI integration. Non-English publications, opinion papers, and duplicated entries were also removed during this phase. 
*   2.**Full-Text Review:** In the second stage, a full-text analysis of the remaining papers was carried out. The studies were evaluated for methodological thoroughness, including the experimental design, validation methods, and compliance with established standards (e.g., ISO/TS 15,066, ISO 10,218). It was also determined how well each work thematically linked to the safety mechanisms, the AI-enhancements, and the industry of collaborative robots. Lastly, the final analysis included any study contributing, empirically or theoretically, to the development of safe, adaptive, and efficient human-robot teaming. 
*   3.**Historical Development of Collaborative Robots** The launch of Intelligent Assist Devices (IADs), patented in the U.S. in 1999, initiated the trend of collaborative robots, also known as cobots. These initial systems operated as computer-controlled tools that enhanced human performance, particularly in terms of safety and efficiency, in areas such as automobile assembly. IADs marked the foundational step in enabling human-robot collaboration without replacing human labor outright [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#bib0001)]. Initial cobots used passive guidance without independent power, prioritizing safety to assist workers. KUKA released the LBR3, a lightweight robotic arm based on the work of the German Aerospace Center (DLR), in 2004 [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#bib0002)]. This was an early milestone in providing a built-in power supply with the ability for advanced motion control, which greatly expanded the flexibility for human-robot interactions and was a responsiveness feature of the new generation. Later models, such as KUKA’s LWR, featured torque control and compliance, allowing for safe use near humans in unpredictable environments [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#bib0003)]. The 2008 launch of Universal Robots' UR5 marked a turning point. Unlike earlier robots that required cages, the UR5 utilized force sensors and real-time feedback for safe human interaction. This made cobots accessible beyond large-scale manufacturing, expanding their use into small and medium-sized enterprises [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#bib0004)]. The UR5 and UR5e offered precise pose control, repeatability, and compliance, enabling use in tasks such as robotic 3D printing with minimal structural modifications [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#bib0005)]. The UR5e exemplifies these trends. It has been utilized in education, particularly with smartphone interfaces, to facilitate easier learning. It has also been applied in research on human-like movements, such as handwriting [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#bib0006)]. Furthermore, it is used in surgical assistance systems that rely on object recognition to address staff shortages [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#bib0007)]. 

A timeline of major technological milestones in cobot evolution is provided in [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#fig0002), illustrating the shift from passive assistance tools to intelligent, autonomous robotic collaborators.

![Image 12: Fig 2:](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S259012302501775X-gr2.jpg)

Fig. 2. Timeline of Historical Development of Collaborative Robots.

Technology has accelerated cobots in four major areas:
*   •**Sensing Technologies:** Today's cobots rely on multiple sensors, such as force-torque sensors, cameras, or proximity sensors, to develop a real-time understanding of their environment. This enables them to adjust and adapt their approach while avoiding hazards and increasing their ability to perform tasks more accurately. Progress in robotic sensing has been primarily focused on Europe, largely through the development of non-destructive testing. As a result, we have already developed significant capabilities with smart sensors that enable the safe and effective inspection of structures. These technological advancements have also helped improve and deepen the level of situational awareness necessary for cobots to be trustworthy in the industry. 
*   •**Control Systems**: Cobots control themselves using complex control algorithms, such as impedance and admittance control, and develop sophisticated reactions to projected external forces, enabling them to work smoothly and safely with humans. A 2021 study utilized tools that integrate modern COBOTS with Industrial Control Systems, such as Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) (e.g., Robot Operating System or ROS) [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#bib0009)], in a collaborative robot deployment within an actual manufacturing context. A compilation of literature was presented at the Warsaw University of Technology that presented how robotic control algorithms were studied and applied to the area of collaborative robots - which included various controller methods, including accuracy in motion control, prediction of user-intended actions, and application of care and safety in user task sharing with robots [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#bib0010)]. This progress is essential for enhancing cobots across industries, ultimately leading to more effective, reliable, and safer interactions between machines and humans. 
*   •**Safety Mechanisms**: They incorporate a suite of safety mechanisms including built-in sensors, redundant safety protocols (e.g., automatic stop functions), soft robotics components, and enforced speed limits. These features are compliant with ISO/TS 15,066, which defines safety standards for human-robot collaboration. Bi et al. (2021) explore these safety dimensions at a systemic level, emphasizing that collaborative manufacturing requires uniform safety assurance across all system components, not just the cobots themselves [[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#bib0011)]. The paper examines techniques for safe interaction in various collaboration modes and emphasizes the importance of a comprehensive approach to safety that encompasses tools, software, and operational protocols. This highlights the need for integrated safety assurance frameworks in complex human-machine work environments. 
*   •**Integration of AI and Machine Learning**: AI and Machine Learning Integration - AI modules enhance the capabilities of cobots in complex, dynamic environments. AI can learn how to accomplish tasks, process visual recognition, and interact with humans in natural language. This level of artificial intelligence enables the cobots to understand the context of the situation and make decisions proactively. Integration with frameworks such as ROS (Robot Operating System) and software like MATLAB/Simulink have increased programmability and versatility. Additionally, Gkournelos et al. demonstrate the potential impact on human-robot collaboration frameworks with the addition of Large Language Models (LLMs). For instance, they propose a Cobot system (CollabAI) that utilizes a fine-tuned GPT AI assistant within augmented-reality interfaces to provide real-time assistance with tasks and communications with robots [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#bib0012)]. The authors reported experiments performed in automotive assembly and machining that showed LLMs as assisting with configuration and positioning in assembly lines. Wang et al. [[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#bib0013)] developed a vision-and-language cobot navigation large language model (LLM) to reduce the number of stoppages in production tasks, such as tool retrieval. The authors' system takes verbal directions to establish a set of Python code that, through module collaboration, generates an autonomous navigation plan using the Pathfinder algorithm in a 3D-reconstructed environment. Their results indicated the potential of LLMs to increase operational efficiencies and enable human-centric capabilities in smart manufacturing. 

The global collaborative robot (cobot) market is projected to reach $11.8 billion by 2030, growing at a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 35.2 % from 2024 to 2030 [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#bib0014)]. This growth is driven by increasing demand for cost-effective, flexible automation in sectors such as automotive, electronics, healthcare, and logistics. Cobots appeal especially to SMEs by improving productivity and safety without requiring protective barriers. In 2024, the automotive industry led the adoption of cobots, particularly in tasks such as assembly and welding. Regionally, the Asia-Pacific accounted for the largest market share (38 %), fueled by industrial growth in China and Japan. Europe and North America are also experiencing strong growth due to Industry 4.0 initiatives and government-backed robotics research [[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#bib0015)]. [Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259012302501775X#fig0003) illustrates the projected global demand trends through 2028.

4. Types of collaborative robots and their applications
-------------------------------------------------------

Cobots represent a significant innovation in automation, enabling close collaboration between humans and robots across various industries. They are categorized based on safety and functionality designed for specific manufacturing, healthcare, and logistics tasks.

### 4.1. Safety features of collaborative robots

#### 4.1.1. AI-Enhanced power and force limiting (PFL)

Power and force-limiting cobots prioritize safety by utilizing sensors to detect human contact and halt movement immediately, thereby preventing injury. Such cobots may operate independently of other safety measures, such as vision systems or fences, once they have been declared safe through risk evaluation. # Human–robot interaction: What changes in the workplace?

The extent of interaction between workers and robots is expected to increase in modern workplaces due to rapid advancements in robotic technologies. Advanced robotics often leverages progress in artificial intelligence, machine learning and sensor technologies to achieve higher levels of sophistication and versatility. The enhanced capabilities of new-generation robots facilitate increased collaboration between humans and robots, partly by ensuring safety when humans and robots are working in proximity. This marks a move away from traditional robots, often confined to cages on the shop floor to isolate them from human operators. In spite of the many benefits, there are lingering concerns around the requirement for workers to continually adapt to new or changing tasks and roles, the possibility of monitoring workers’ activities at an unprecedented level of granularity, diminished autonomy and control over the pace of work, and the emergence of new health and safety risks, including of a psychosocial nature. Drawing on survey data and case studies investigating advanced robotic systems and applications, this report explores the opportunities and challenges that come with closer human–robot interaction, with a view to contributing to the broader policy debate on the automation of work.

[**![Digital story: Human–robot interaction: What changed in the workplace?](/sites/default/files/inline-images/ef23010-c-greenbutterfly-adobe-stock-crop_0.png)**](https://www.eurofound.europa.eu/en/resources/data-story/2024/human-robot-interaction-what-changes-workplace "Digital story: Human–robot interaction: What changes in the workplace?") **Explore the** [**Digital story: Human–robot interaction: What changes in the workplace?**](https://www.eurofound.europa.eu/en/resources/data-story/2024/human-robot-interaction-what-changes-workplace "Digital story: Human–robot interaction: What changes in the workplace?")

![Digital story: Human–robot interaction: What changed in the workplace?](/sites/default/files/inline-images/ef23010-c-greenbutterfly-adobe-stock-crop_0.png)

[Human–robot interaction: What changes in the workplace?](https://www.eurofound.europa.eu/sites/default/files/2024-07/ef23010en.pdf)

## Key messages

## Executive summary

Advanced robotic systems and applications disrupt workplaces; they transform the way work is carried out, often resulting in changes to business models and redefining roles, tasks and methods of work. Artificial intelligence (AI) has played a pivotal role in enhancing these systems, giving them greater capabilities, functionalities and flexibility than more conventional robots. AI has also facilitated seamless collaboration and interaction between humans and robots in various industries. This is most prominently illustrated by collaborative robotic applications, through which AI enables closer worker–robot interaction in shared workspaces.

As advanced robots become more complex and prevalent in modern work environments, understanding how workers and robots interact and the implications for work organisation and working conditions is crucial for robots’ successful integration into the workplace. The changes brought about by autonomous or semi-autonomous advanced robotics require thoughtful consideration and proactive management to ensure a positive impact on businesses and workers.

Drawing on survey data and case studies investigating advanced robotic systems and applications for task automation, this report contributes to the policy debate on work automation, highlighting new forms of interaction between workers and robots and the changes to work organisation and working conditions that they entail.

### **Policy context**

Unlike conventional robots, usually confined to cages and positioned at a safe distance from humans, advanced robots equipped with sensors and enhanced functionalities ensure greater safety when in proximity to humans. They enable closer human–robot interaction and collaboration characterised by shared goals and more synchronised tasks. Despite these benefits, the forms of interaction arising from the use of increasingly advanced robotic systems, especially those with embedded AI capabilities, may pose new policy and regulatory challenges. The notion of human centricity is more relevant than ever in ensuring safe and effective human–robot interaction.

As part of the EU’s digital strategy, several policy initiatives on AI have emphasised a human-centric approach to technology development and use. In the context of advanced robotics, this involves ensuring that systems are designed and deployed in a manner that respects human values and fundamental rights. Furthermore, the European Commission has taken steps to address liability and accountability issues arising from AI use, proposing a revised product liability directive and an AI civil liability directive.

Safety is another important concern in human–robot interaction; with the EU’s adoption in 2023 of the new European Machinery Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2023/1230), it sought to update the safety requirements for machinery (and related products) on the EU market. This regulation acknowledges that safety risks extend beyond physical damage to psychological stress. However, the primary legal instrument governing matters of occupational safety and health (OSH) remains the OSH Framework Directive (89/391/EEC).

Other key challenges arising from interaction with advanced robots relate to skills requirements. For some time, the EU has recognised the impact of technological advancements on the labour market and the skills the workforce requires. In this regard, a major policy initiative is the European Skills Agenda for sustainable competitiveness, social fairness and resilience. The agenda encompasses several actions focusing on upskilling and reskilling to ensure individuals have the skills necessary for current and future job markets.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Innovative technologies like AI, machine learning, and advanced safety systems enhance human-robot collaboration, enabling real-time adaptability and improved safety measures in shared workspaces.
Ongoing R&D focuses on refining collaborative robots' safety features, integrating smart sensors and AI to minimize risks in human-robot interactions and enhance operational efficiency.
As safety standards improve and costs decrease, industries are increasingly adopting collaborative robots, leading to enhanced productivity and safer work environments across various sectors.
